title: Self Deportation

jokes.text: Ron Howard wants to make a heartfelt father-son drama that has a good clean message for his illegitimate daughter to star in.
jokes.setup: Ron Howard is described as wanting to make a wholesome family film with a good clean message
jokes.punchline: ...for his illegitimate daughter to star in — undercutting the 'wholesome' framing instantly
jokes.explanation: The joke is tightly constructed: the setup sells wholesomeness, the punchline punctures it with a single specific detail. The illegitimate daughter is a real Ron Howard fact the show weaponizes. Economy is excellent — it's one clause. Rewatch bonus because you appreciate the layering on re-view.

jokes.text: You have to crank out family dramas before they let you make your dream superhero movie. Do you have a dream superhero movie? I want to remake Spider-Man 2, the first one.
jokes.setup: Kitty explains the Hollywood system of making family dramas to earn superhero credits
jokes.punchline: Her dream is to remake Spider-Man 2... 'the first one' — a self-contradicting, incoherent statement delivered with full confidence
jokes.explanation: The contradiction 'Spider-Man 2, the first one' is a perfectly absurdist non-sequitur that reveals character — Kitty is an idiot in positions of power, a great recurring AD type. Highly quotable. The economy is sharp; the joke lands in four words after the setup.

jokes.text: My brother Buster has something better than a hook. A robot hand.
jokes.setup: Michael pitches Buster as the subject of the movie, noting he has 'something better than a hook'
jokes.punchline: A robot hand — which is functionally the same as a hook, just rebranded, and Michael says it with complete sincerity
jokes.explanation: The joke works on multiple levels for long-time viewers: Buster's hook, the Army issuing it, and now Michael casually pitching it as a selling point. The character integration is high — only Michael would earnestly pitch his family's dysfunction as a movie hook.

jokes.text: If she could go that unlikable.
jokes.setup: Kitty suggests Rebel Alley could play Lucille in the movie
jokes.punchline: Michael's aside — 'if she could go that unlikable' — is a backhanded dig at his own mother delivered with complete casual understatement
jokes.explanation: Six words that efficiently summarize Lucille's character and Michael's complicated feelings toward her. Economy is exceptional. Character integration is strong — Michael's passive-aggressive relationship with Lucille is baked in.

jokes.text: They're Imagine dollars. They're redeemable in the gift shop. That's Ron Howard. Brian's on the 50.
jokes.setup: Michael is told he'll get his $500 severance, implying real money
jokes.punchline: It's Imagine Entertainment novelty currency with Ron Howard's face on the bill and Brian Grazer on the 50
jokes.explanation: This is genuinely excellent AD-style absurdist comedy: a real production company has its own vanity currency, and the specific detail of Grazer being on the 50 is the perfect escalating absurdist grace note. It's original, densely structured, and rewards attention. Justifies the 8+ score.

jokes.text: It's a severance thing they're doing because of Ron's need to be loved and Brian's conflict avoidance issues.
jokes.setup: The existence of Imagine dollars has just been established
jokes.punchline: The narrator psychoanalyzes the real Ron Howard and Brian Grazer as if they're recurring sitcom characters with documented neuroses
jokes.explanation: The narrator casually applying pop-psychology diagnoses to real showbiz figures is a meta joke that works because of the show's unique position — Ron Howard actually narrates this show. The layering rewards rewatching.

jokes.text: I think I'm gonna take one Gung Ho kimono, okay? Oh, and maybe a Brian wig. That's fun. Oh, we only sell these as a set.
jokes.setup: Michael browses the Imagine Entertainment gift shop
jokes.punchline: The shop sells a 'Brian wig' (Grazer's signature spiky hair), and a kimono tied to a Ron Howard film — and they're sold as a set, implying this is a curated merchandise experience
jokes.explanation: Escalates the Imagine dollars absurdity — not just currency but a full branded merchandise ecosystem. The Brian Grazer wig is a sharp specific detail. Slightly bloated in execution but the payoff of 'sold as a set' is a good button.

jokes.text: You've reached George Maharis. Did you want that Rebel Alley hair? Oh. Uh, no no. That's... that is mine. I came with that.
jokes.setup: Michael calls his son and gets voicemail — George Michael is using the alias 'George Maharis'
jokes.punchline: The gift shop employee asks if Michael wants the Rebel Alley hair extension, implying he looks like he's wearing movie merchandise — and he defensively clarifies it's his real hair
jokes.explanation: Two jokes compressed into one moment: George Michael's alias on voicemail (a callback that rewards fans) and the hair confusion that plays on Michael's vanity. The defense 'I came with that' is a perfect embarrassed understatement.

jokes.text: Winner of 1982's Least Seen on Campus.
jokes.setup: Buster says he's a 'Milford man' — referring to the Milford Academy whose motto is 'children should be seen and not heard'
jokes.punchline: He won an award for being the least visible student — an award that perfectly encapsulates the absurd institutional logic of Milford
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional AD joke construction: it takes a long-running gag (Milford Academy) and adds a new specific detail that perfectly encapsulates the absurdity — a competitive award for invisibility. The character integration is near-perfect; only Buster would have this credential and be proud of it. Buster then adds 'I wish I could've seen their faces at the ceremony' — an impossibility within the joke's own logic that elevates it further. Justifies 8+ score.

jokes.text: I wish I could've seen their faces at the ceremony.
jokes.setup: Buster just said he won 'Least Seen on Campus' at Milford
jokes.punchline: He wishes he could've seen the faces at the ceremony — which by Milford logic, no one would have been visible at, making it a logical impossibility that Buster is oblivious to
jokes.explanation: This is the best joke in the episode and a genuine standout moment in the series. The internal logic is airtight: if everyone at Milford is unseen, then the award ceremony itself would have been invisible, making it literally impossible to see anyone's faces. Buster's wistful delivery of an impossible desire captures his tragic obliviousness perfectly. Economy is flawless — seven words. Character integration is as high as it gets; no other character could say this. Comparable to the vasectomy anchor example in terms of earned escalation and character specificity. Justifies 9+ score.

jokes.text: show your hand / This is one of only two things the Army warns us about when they issue these.
jokes.setup: Michael tells Buster he has to 'make sure you don't show your hand'
jokes.punchline: Buster takes it literally and refers to his robot hand, noting the Army specifically warned him about it
jokes.explanation: The wordplay on 'show your hand' is elegantly integrated with Buster's literal-mindedness and his robot hand. The payoff — that the Army has a warning specifically about this — is a perfect absurdist button. The 'one of only two things' implies a tantalizing mystery about the other warning.

jokes.text: Maybe that's a role I can play in your life. Don't say... Mom. Mmm.
jokes.setup: Michael says he was 'his son's mom and dad for so many years'; Buster offers to fill a role in Michael's life
jokes.punchline: Michael cuts him off preemptively saying 'don't say' — but Buster says 'Mom' not 'Dad', revealing Buster's Oedipal fixation and making it far worse
jokes.explanation: Beautiful misdirection: the audience and Michael expect 'Dad,' which would be a gentle, slightly touching response. 'Mom' is far more disturbing and perfectly on-brand for Buster's mother obsession. The structure is near-perfect — Michael's preemptive 'don't say' makes the reveal land harder. Character integration is excellent: only Buster would offer to be someone's 'mom.'

jokes.text: I thought you were gonna say Dad.
jokes.setup: Buster just said 'Mom' after Michael's preemptive 'don't say'
jokes.punchline: Michael's flat, deflated response confirms how much worse 'Mom' is than 'Dad' would have been
jokes.explanation: A clean button on joke #12. Works as a reaction beat — the flat delivery communicates Michael's resigned horror. Economy is excellent. Less original on its own but integral to the two-beat structure.

jokes.text: Well, I lived with Mother. Did you think we were dating?
jokes.setup: Michael points out Buster 'lived with' Lucille 2, implying a romantic relationship
jokes.punchline: Buster uses the exact same logic ('I lived with Mother') to deny romance with Lucille 2 — completely oblivious that this analogy makes things far, far worse
jokes.explanation: One of the strongest jokes in the episode. Buster's logic is airtight from his perspective and catastrophically broken from everyone else's. The structure is nearly perfect: he uses a comparison to prove innocence that simultaneously proves guilt of something far more disturbing. Only Buster could deliver this with utter sincerity. Comparable to the 8+ anchor example in density and character specificity.

jokes.text: Question's been asked. [reaction beat — the narrator's deadpan acknowledgment]
jokes.setup: Buster has just compared his relationship with Lucille 2 to his relationship with his mother
jokes.punchline: The narrator's four-word non-comment is the only possible response — moving on without processing what just happened
jokes.explanation: Four words that somehow say everything. The narrator's refusal to engage is itself the joke — and the economy is perfect. The phrase is quotable and captures the show's unique voice. The narrator as comedy performer rather than just expository device is quintessential AD.

jokes.text: Should I not say that?
jokes.setup: Buster has just accidentally confessed to knowing details about the night Lucille 2 disappeared, including seeing her 'lifeless body'
jokes.punchline: His only concern after this potentially incriminating statement is social etiquette, not self-preservation
jokes.explanation: Perfect Buster moment: socially oblivious in the exact wrong direction. He's worried about manners while potentially confessing to a crime. Character integration is excellent — this is pure Buster.

jokes.text: What? In person? Yeah. Uh, no. On the phone.
jokes.setup: Michael says he 'spoke to Lucille 2 today'
jokes.punchline: He immediately corrects 'in person' to 'on the phone' — but the hesitation after 'yeah' implies Michael may actually know more than he's letting on about Lucille 2's fate
jokes.explanation: A dark misdirection beat that plays as slight discomfort on first watch but gains weight on rewatch — is Michael lying? The hesitation is a carefully constructed piece of the season's mystery. Rewatch bonus is high.

jokes.text: Just Mama's worst nightmare. Buster's missing. Oh, and his bath has gone cold.
jokes.setup: Michael is framing Buster's 'disappearance' as something to scare Lucille into coming home
jokes.punchline: Lucille's worst nightmare is not her son being missing — it's also his bath going cold, placing both concerns on equal footing
jokes.explanation: The cold bath as equal nightmare to her son's disappearance is a perfect encapsulation of Lucille's parenting priorities. Economy is sharp. Character integration for Lucille is excellent.

jokes.text: Maybe have a full psych panel while you're in there, too. Oh, those things are so easy to beat.
jokes.setup: Michael suggests Buster get a psych evaluation at the police station
jokes.punchline: Buster confidently says psych panels are 'so easy to beat' — coming from a man who has just potentially confessed to knowing where a missing woman's body is
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy that works on two levels: Buster's breezy confidence is funny on its own, and terrifying given what we're beginning to suspect. The irony is rich — the least self-aware person in the show thinks he can fool psychiatrists.

jokes.text: His recent rivalry with magician Tony Wonder had blossomed into a kind of friendship... before contracting into a rivalry again. Same. Which suddenly became a... oh, let's say, boarding school romance.
jokes.setup: The narrator describes G.O.B.'s complicated relationship with Tony Wonder using a series of evolving metaphors
jokes.punchline: After dancing around it with 'friendship,' 'rivalry,' the narrator settles on 'boarding school romance' — a euphemism that simultaneously implies gay romance and deniability. 'Same' is G.O.B.'s internal confirmation.
jokes.explanation: 'Oh, let's say, boarding school romance' is the narrator actively searching for a phrase that's accurate but deniable — a meta-joke about how the show handles G.O.B.'s sexuality. 'Same' as G.O.B.'s private confirmation is perfectly economical. Character integration is near-perfect.

jokes.text: Uh, this place is full of cincos and mayos. We need to find out whatever the word for ten is. Find that. It's ten. Every culture uses ten.
jokes.setup: G.O.B. complains the bar is full of low-rated women using the Cinco de Mayo date as a rating scale (5s and 6s)
jokes.punchline: He wants to find a 'ten' but doesn't know the Spanish word for it; George Sr. points out '10 is ten' in every language — which G.O.B. receives as a revelation
jokes.explanation: Brilliant wordplay: 'cincos and mayos' as a rating system built from Cinco de Mayo is a layered pun that works on multiple levels simultaneously (objectification, G.O.B.'s shallow character, wordplay). The callback that 'ten' is universal is a perfect button. Highly quotable and a standout AD wordplay moment.

jokes.text: I can get hard, so let's focus on you.
jokes.setup: The therapist is working on George Sr.'s sexual feeling through traditional Hopi medicine, stimulating a pressure point
jokes.punchline: The therapist's clinical report — 'I can get hard, so let's focus on you' — is devastatingly ambiguous about whether he means his own arousal or something else
jokes.explanation: The double entendre is sharp and the clinical framing makes it land harder. 'Let's focus on you' is pitch-perfect as an offhand deflection of an awkward fact. Economy is excellent.

jokes.text: They're primas. Primos, huh? I've gotta be honest, I've seen better. No, it means cousins. One hates her father, and the other hates her grandfather.
jokes.setup: Women send drinks to G.O.B. and George Sr.; the waitress says they're 'primas' (cousins in Spanish)
jokes.punchline: G.O.B. mishears as a rating ('primos, huh, I've seen better'), then when corrected learns these women specifically hate the generation of men he represents — their own fathers and grandfathers
jokes.explanation: Beautifully layered: G.O.B.'s misunderstanding is funny; the correction that they're cousins (a callback to George Michael/Maeby's situation) is funny; the specification that one hates her father (George Sr.) and one hates her grandfather (G.O.B. would be a grandfather's age) is a devastating character joke. Three levels of comedy in rapid succession.

jokes.text: I don't know about you, but I'd prefer the thrill of the hunt, as opposed to... If we wait, something a little better will come along. Thank you. Not trying to fall head over heels here. Not super eager to be, uh, missing somebody. I mean, who you love.
jokes.setup: G.O.B. and George Sr. explain why they're not pursuing the available women
jokes.punchline: Both men articulate their rejections in language that's obviously about romantic longing — G.O.B. for Tony Wonder, George Sr. for someone — while pretending to talk about casual dating strategy
jokes.explanation: The deflection through macho framing is transparent and sad in a way that's characteristically AD — men pretending not to have feelings while clearly having enormous feelings. 'Missing somebody. I mean, who you love' is a stumbling confession that G.O.B. doesn't quite complete.

jokes.text: APB. 'Aw, poor Buster.'
jokes.setup: Michael mentions that after 48 hours they'll put out an APB on Buster
jokes.punchline: Buster immediately decodes 'APB' as 'Aw, poor Buster' — which is not what APB means but is exactly what Buster would want it to mean
jokes.explanation: Exceptional joke. 'APB' decoded as 'Aw, poor Buster' is a perfect AD wordplay construction — it's not random, it's character-specific. Of course Buster would interpret a police acronym through the lens of maternal sympathy. The economy is perfect; the character integration couldn't be higher. Quotability is among the highest in the episode. Justifies 8+ score.

jokes.text: [Buster laughs] What's so funny? You always come back to save the family. We joke about that all the time.
jokes.setup: Michael says he doesn't know if he'll come back this time
jokes.punchline: Buster laughs at the very concept — it's literally the premise of the entire show, and Buster calling it out as an in-universe joke is meta without breaking the fourth wall completely
jokes.explanation: A gentle meta joke that acknowledges the show's own structure without fully breaking the fourth wall. Works best for fans who recognize the series-long pattern. Rewatch bonus because you appreciate the self-awareness more over time.

jokes.text: Buster's extended Michael impression: 'Hi, I'm Michael, and I save everyone so I feel special, even though you don't deserve it. I'm leaving. I won't be coming back. Look who's back.'
jokes.setup: Michael is trying to explain to Buster that this time he's really leaving
jokes.punchline: Buster does a rapid-fire impression of Michael's entire recurring arc — leave, come back, save everyone — in about 15 seconds, collapsing multiple seasons of character behavior into a savage parody
jokes.explanation: Buster's impression is scathing and accurate — and delivered by the most unlikely character to have this meta-awareness. The speed of the impression (he blows through multiple seasons of character arc in seconds) is itself a joke about how repetitive Michael's behavior is. Justifies 8+ with character specificity and structural cleverness.

jokes.text: Covered a lot of ground there.
jokes.setup: Buster has just done a 30-second impression that covered Michael's entire character arc AND a compressed Lucille/Buster dynamic
jokes.punchline: Michael's only response is the understated acknowledgment 'covered a lot of ground there' — accepting the impression's accuracy with weary resignation
jokes.explanation: Perfect button on Buster's impression. The economy is exceptional — four words that convey Michael's resigned self-awareness. Works as a reaction beat that confirms rather than denies the impression's accuracy.

jokes.text: Okay, like, bad example, I saw Lucille 2's lifeless body sprawled across the stair car?
jokes.setup: Michael is coaching Buster on how to answer police questions honestly; Buster asks what kind of detail he means
jokes.punchline: Buster's 'bad example' of what NOT to say is an extremely specific and incriminating statement about Lucille 2's body — which he delivers as if it's a hypothetical
jokes.explanation: Devastating dark comedy. Buster's framing of this as a 'bad example' to NOT say is precisely what makes it land — he's accidentally confessing while trying to demonstrate what not to confess. The stair car is a perfect specific AD detail. On rewatch the weight of this statement is immense.

jokes.text: Perfect. If that's what happened, tell them.
jokes.setup: Buster has just described seeing Lucille 2's lifeless body as a 'bad example' of what not to say
jokes.punchline: Michael says 'perfect' — completely missing the implication that this might be a confession, not a hypothetical
jokes.explanation: Michael's blithe 'perfect' is terrifying on rewatch. He's so focused on his plan that he fails to register what Buster is telling him. The economy is flawless. Dark comedy elevated by dramatic irony.

jokes.text: She's gonna be as dry as, uh... well, Lucille 1. I can hear Mother winking now.
jokes.setup: Michael says Lucille 2 will be back from whatever 'bender' she's on, and will be as dry as...
jokes.punchline: He catches himself — Lucille 1 is an alcoholic, so 'dry' is ironic; Buster adds that he can 'hear Mother winking' at the comparison
jokes.explanation: A layered irony joke: 'dry' applied to Lucille 1 (an alcoholic) and Lucille 2 (actually dry due to vertigo medication) is sharp. 'I can hear Mother winking' is a characteristic Buster moment — he hears his absent mother's physical gestures.

jokes.text: You already got into the candy bears, huh? This is literally one of only two things the Army warns about when they issue these.
jokes.setup: Michael has left groceries for Buster; he notices Buster has already eaten the candy
jokes.punchline: Buster says this is 'literally one of only two things the Army warns about when they issue these' — referring to his robot hand, using the exact same phrase he used earlier when told not to show his hand
jokes.explanation: The callback to 'one of only two things the Army warns about' is executed cleanly — the same phrase in a completely different context reveals new information (one warning is about candy) while being absurdly specific. The running gag about the Army's warnings is a perfect AD construction.

jokes.text: You already got into the candy bears, huh? / This is literally one of only two things the Army warns about when they issue these.
jokes.setup: The robot hand's Army warnings were established earlier
jokes.punchline: The second Army warning is: don't eat candy bears with the robot hand. The specificity of this warning and Buster's guilty acknowledgment completes the running gag.
jokes.explanation: Excellent payoff to the running gag. The specificity that one of only two Army warnings for the robot hand involves candy bears is perfect Arrested Development absurdism. Buster already having violated it (implicitly) makes it funnier. The callback is well-earned.

jokes.text: Hi, it's Lucille Austero. I've been looking for you. Note, I mean. I know, I'd left you a note... This is why you always... leave a note.
jokes.setup: Michael hears a voicemail from Lucille Austero at George Michael's door
jokes.punchline: The narrator delivers 'This is why you always leave a note' — the iconic early-series motto, triggered by Michael finding a note from his son instead of his son in person
jokes.explanation: A beloved callback that lands primarily because of its long history in the show. The organic way it's triggered (a missed connection because of a voicemail/note) is well-constructed. Impact is high for established fans. 'Where's that from?' joke acknowledges new viewers while rewarding veterans.

jokes.text: Hi, it's Lucille Austero. — No! — I've been looking for you. / Note, I mean.
jokes.setup: Michael goes to see his son and finds a note — hears a voicemail as he approaches
jokes.punchline: What sounds like Lucille 2 speaking to Michael is revealed to be a recorded voicemail/note she left — 'No!' is Michael's shocked reaction to hearing her voice, then 'Note, I mean' corrects the impression
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection — the audience briefly thinks Lucille 2 is alive and confronting Michael, then it's corrected to a note. Given the mystery of Lucille 2's disappearance, this lands with extra tension/comedy.

jokes.text: Where's that from?
jokes.setup: The narrator says 'always leave a note' — the show's famous motto
jokes.punchline: Michael himself asks where the phrase is from — he's in the show but doesn't recognize his own show's motto, a meta joke about the show's cult status
jokes.explanation: A cheeky meta moment — Michael doesn't know his own catchphrase. Works as a wink to the audience. Economy is excellent. Slightly less earned than a full joke but a sharp aside.

jokes.text: Oh, so much for apologizing. I'm telling you, he's moved on. Okay? You should, too.
jokes.setup: George Michael arrives to apologize to Michael and finds he's gone; Maeby tells him to move on
jokes.punchline: Maeby, the most manipulative character in the show, is giving George Michael relationship advice — specifically the advice that benefits her (him staying with her instead of reconciling with his dad)
jokes.explanation: The irony works because we know Maeby's motives are self-serving. Not a spectacular joke but solid character work. Rewatch bonus because her manipulation is clearer in retrospect.

jokes.text: Welcome to the shitty parents club. No, no, we don't have kids. No.
jokes.setup: George Michael says his dad went to Mexico without telling him; Maeby says 'welcome to the shitty parents club'
jokes.punchline: George Michael and Maeby simultaneously correct themselves — they don't have kids; they meant they HAVE shitty parents, but also they are not parents of each other despite their complicated history
jokes.explanation: A quick, clean joke that plays on the ambiguity of 'we' in 'we don't have kids.' Their immediate correction adds a layer — they're denying both the literal meaning and possibly the metaphorical one. Efficient.

jokes.text: Just the cousins. Mm-hmm. Which turned out to be... Primos! This crew.
jokes.setup: George Michael and Maeby agree to go to Mexico together — 'just the cousins'
jokes.punchline: The narrator cuts to the full group of Noah-clones they end up with, while G.O.B.'s earlier 'Primos!' is repurposed as the title card for this group
jokes.explanation: A structural joke — the narrator undercuts 'just the cousins' with the reality of the group they acquire. The callback to 'primos' ties together the episode's Mexico plots neatly.

jokes.text: Michael was also heading to Mexico. And after taking care of one last bit of business... Uh, yeah, I'm completely sure he's been gone 72 hours. As a matter of fact, his bath has grown cold. So that's, uh, an alarm bell right there.
jokes.setup: Michael calls in Buster as missing using the details they established
jokes.punchline: Michael delivers the cold bath as a forensic alarm bell in the most matter-of-fact way — making an absurd detail sound serious
jokes.explanation: The callback to the cold bath works because Michael's delivery makes it sound like an actual CSI clue. The absurdity of 'an alarm bell right there' applied to bath temperature is consistently funny.

jokes.text: No pictures, thank you! Put your phones down. We take the pictures. When the fleet of recovery vans arrived from the search engine Michael had been working for, he discovered that the car that takes the pictures was no longer under his control.
jokes.setup: Michael is trying to flee in the Google Maps-style camera car
jokes.punchline: Random bystanders are trying to photograph the camera car; Michael — who works for a company that photographs everything — demands privacy while driving a car whose entire purpose is photographing others
jokes.explanation: Sharp situational irony — the surveillance photographer demanding not to be photographed. The specificity of the setup (this is clearly Google Street View) makes it land. 'We take the pictures' is a good punchline.

jokes.text: Hi, I'm Dave from Mapping. She's Lisa from Traditional Search. And that's Jeff. I'm Jeff from Earth.
jokes.setup: Search employees arrive to reclaim Michael's car
jokes.punchline: After normal introductions ('Dave from Mapping,' 'Lisa from Traditional Search'), the last person introduces himself as 'Jeff from Earth' — as if 'Earth' is a department within the company
jokes.explanation: Classic AD absurdist construction: establish a pattern (department introductions), then break it with something impossibly large. 'From Earth' as a corporate department is both a company name joke (Google Earth) and an absurdist escalation. Economy is perfect.

jokes.text: We heard we had a runaway ostrich.
jokes.setup: The Search employees explain why they came
jokes.punchline: They tracked Michael's car and arrived — but their stated reason is 'a runaway ostrich,' which is either corporate jargon for a rogue driver or simply a non-sequitur — the show doesn't explain it
jokes.explanation: The unexplained 'runaway ostrich' is an AD-style absurd detail that may or may not mean something. It's funny as pure non-sequitur but the lack of payoff slightly diminishes it. Rewatch bonus if it pays off later in the season.

jokes.text: Oh, no. They never arrest the drivers. They do come down pretty hard on the cars, but it's cheaper to just retrain the people instead. It sounds super futuristic and dystopian, but it's really not. Earth was right.
jokes.setup: Michael asks if he's under arrest for taking the car
jokes.punchline: The correction — they punish the CARS, not the people — and the narrator's confirmation 'Earth was right' (validating 'Jeff from Earth') is a perfectly timed button
jokes.explanation: The absurdist corporate logic of punishing cars over people is a sharp tech-company satire joke. 'Earth was right' is a clean callback button that rewards the 'Jeff from Earth' setup. Solid construction.

jokes.text: Now, if you'll excuse us, we must say buenos días. Vamos, vamos. Play the guitar, old man! Strum! Strum!
jokes.setup: The elder delivers a stern, threatening speech to George Michael and friends about leaving the land, then exits with his group
jokes.punchline: His commanding exit is immediately undercut by him demanding that someone play guitar — 'Strum! Strum!' — deflating the menace completely
jokes.explanation: The bathos of 'Strum! Strum!' after a dramatic threat is well-timed. The character shift from menacing to fussy is a good comedic turn. Solid but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Who were those scary, handsome Mexicans on those magical horses? They just looked like old white men to me.
jokes.setup: The horsemen have just ridden off dramatically
jokes.punchline: George Michael romanticizes them; his companion sees them as they actually are — old white men on normal horses
jokes.explanation: A sharp puncturing of George Michael's tendency to romanticize. The contrast between his elevated language and the flat realism of the response is the joke. Solid if unspectacular.

jokes.text: I was gonna have to leave early, and I didn't know how to tell you. But now we can just all go together.
jokes.setup: The threatening horsemen have just told everyone to leave and never return
jokes.punchline: The companion seizes on the forced expulsion as a convenient solution to his pre-existing awkward social problem — having to leave early
jokes.explanation: The bathos of treating a threatening expulsion as social convenience is a good character joke — this person's primary concern is the awkward social situation of leaving early, not being threatened by armed men on horses.

jokes.text: I just know that you don't like standing up to people on horses with guns. No, I never really was that guy.
jokes.setup: Maeby notices George Michael didn't push back against the horsemen
jokes.punchline: George Michael's resigned 'No, I never really was that guy' is a perfectly delivered self-assessment — he accepts his cowardice with complete equanimity
jokes.explanation: A good character joke that captures George Michael's neurotic self-awareness. The specificity of 'people on horses with guns' makes Maeby's read funny; George Michael's total acceptance makes it land.

jokes.text: Fakeblock doesn't actually exist. Are you telling me that the software that's supposed to keep people's computers unhackable and keep any kind of information private doesn't exist? How did you find out? It was easy. I just looked in your computer under 'Secrets.'
jokes.setup: George Michael tells Maeby that Fakeblock, his privacy software, doesn't exist
jokes.punchline: Maeby discovered this by looking in his computer folder labeled 'Secrets' — the irony being that his privacy software company's biggest secret is stored in a folder called 'Secrets' on his computer
jokes.explanation: The joke has perfect layered irony: a privacy company has its secrets in a folder called 'Secrets.' The structure builds perfectly: devastating reveal → disbelieving question → deadpan explanation. 'Under Secrets' is the punchline. Justifies 8+ for originality of construction and the satisfying meta-irony.

jokes.text: Oh, you looked in my 'Please Don't Read' folder? No, that one was empty. I found it in 'Just Odds and Ends.' Great, you got past my double bluff.
jokes.setup: Maeby says she found Fakeblock's nonexistence in the 'Secrets' folder
jokes.punchline: George Michael's security system is a series of folder names: 'Please Don't Read' (empty — the bluff), 'Just Odds and Ends' (contains the actual secrets), making 'Secrets' just the first layer of a deeply amateur security protocol
jokes.explanation: This is one of the best jokes in the episode. The escalating security theater ('Please Don't Read' → 'Just Odds and Ends') is genius comedy writing about George Michael's character: a privacy software developer who thinks folder names are security. 'Great, you got past my double bluff' is a perfect, slightly defeated button. The structure builds through two beats to a punchline that rewards attention. Justifies 8+ score.

jokes.text: Get going with my own goop. Goop? What is goop? If you have to ask, you probably don't know what it is. I don't know what it is. I feel like maybe you don't know what it is. Maybe it's 'go-op'? To me, that would make less sense. Maybe that's what 'go-op' is. Not everything always making sense.
jokes.setup: George Michael says he wants to 'get going with my own goop' — presumably meaning Gwyneth Paltrow's wellness brand as a model for something
jokes.punchline: Neither of them knows what goop is; George Michael's attempt to define it collapses into 'maybe it's go-op'; the conversation ends with the observation 'not everything always making sense' — delivered as wisdom
jokes.explanation: The goop conversation works as a portrait of two people confidently discussing something neither understands — a very Gen-Y/millennial joke. 'Not everything always making sense' as a life philosophy is a good button. Slightly meandering but the comedic payoff is real.

jokes.text: Meanwhile, George Sr. and G.O.B. continued trying to out-goop each other.
jokes.setup: The goop conversation has just happened
jokes.punchline: The narrator uses 'goop' as a verb to describe George Sr. and G.O.B.'s competitive masculine posturing — connecting two storylines with a single word in a way that recontextualizes both
jokes.explanation: The narrator's ability to bridge storylines with a single repurposed word is quintessential AD craft. 'Out-goop each other' takes a meaningless word and gives it perfect meaning — masculine performance and wellness culture become the same absurdity. A demonstration of the show's exceptional economy.

jokes.text: I won't tell Mom about all the ladies you're if you don't tell Mom about all the ladies I'm.
jokes.setup: G.O.B. and George Sr. make a deal about keeping secrets from Lucille
jokes.punchline: Both sentences are grammatically incomplete — '...you're' and '...I'm' trail off because neither man has actually been with any ladies, making the boast impossible to complete
jokes.explanation: Structural brilliance: the sentences can't be completed because the activity being referenced didn't happen. The grammatical fragment IS the joke. Both characters try to save face while accidentally revealing they have nothing to save face about. Economy is perfect. This is AD writing at its best — the form of the sentence carries the comedy.

jokes.text: She's got a friend! That's a friend? Bush. Trash bag and bush. Thought it was a woman. Yeah. Like, hunched over, right? Yeah. Like, with the... I wanted to do her so bad.
jokes.setup: G.O.B. and George Sr. discover the hot woman has a friend; they look expectantly
jokes.punchline: The 'friend' is a trash bag caught in a bush — which both men have been treating as an attractive woman, and G.O.B. admits he 'wanted to do her so bad'
jokes.explanation: The slow reveal — bush, trash bag, 'I wanted to do her so bad' — is well-structured. G.O.B.'s admission is the perfect button. Works as physical comedy (visual gag inferred) and character comedy. Consistent with the running joke of their failed 'heterosexual vigor.'

jokes.text: That was a ten-gallon Hefty sack? Oh, yeah. Yeah, a ten. Definitely a ten. Yeah. Nice. I like... a ten.
jokes.setup: The trash bag has just been revealed; both men are recovering from the embarrassment
jokes.punchline: They revert to rating the trash bag as a 'ten' using the 'ten' rating system established in the cincos/mayos joke — the callback reinforces their delusion and the running gag simultaneously
jokes.explanation: The callback to 'ten' while rating a trash bag is a clean construction. The men's inability to admit mistake drives them to retroactively rate the bag as a perfect ten. 'I like... a ten' as G.O.B.'s trailing acknowledgment is a good understated button.

jokes.text: Lucille, at the behest of her court-mandated therapist, was struggling to make sense of things. Am I horrible for saying these things? No. But I was doing the math, and I still think you're gonna have a problem with this final 'raped murderer.'
jokes.setup: Lucille is in therapy with Tobias; she's apparently been listing people she has issues with
jokes.punchline: Tobias's therapeutic concern isn't about her emotional state — it's about the taxonomy issue with her final entry, a 'raped murderer.' He's doing the MATH on her list of grievances
jokes.explanation: Tobias's therapeutic incompetence is perfectly captured — he's focused on the wrong thing entirely (math? taxonomy?) while the patient clearly needs validation. 'Raped murderer' as a category he's taking seriously rather than questioning is deeply funny dark comedy.

jokes.text: How many more sessions do I have to endure until I'm done with... no offense... you? Legally, two. But I am in the family. I mean... this is my family.
jokes.setup: Lucille asks how many more sessions she has to attend
jokes.punchline: Tobias answers 'legally, two' — then pivots to claim family membership to extend his access; the pause before 'this is my family' reveals him recalibrating his argument on the fly
jokes.explanation: Tobias's desperation to remain relevant to the Bluths is perfectly captured. 'Legally, two' is a clean, clinical answer that makes his subsequent family claim funnier. The pause before 'this is my family' is great character comedy.

jokes.text: My daughter is divorcing you. This is it. I'm confused by the word 'it.' Are you implying that you don't want to see me anymore? I'm confused by the word 'anymore.'
jokes.setup: Lucille tells Tobias her daughter is divorcing him and 'this is it'
jokes.punchline: Tobias picks apart individual words ('confused by the word it'; 'confused by the word anymore') as a therapeutic deflection — using linguistic analysis to avoid the obvious
jokes.explanation: Exceptional character comedy. Tobias's wordplay is his most authentic self — he uses therapeutic language as a shield against emotional reality. 'I'm confused by the word anymore' is perfect because 'anymore' implies there WAS a time she wanted to see him, which he's desperate to hold onto. Character integration is as high as it gets for Tobias. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: You've lost touch with your daughter. Put me in, for God's sake.
jokes.setup: Tobias is arguing his case for staying connected to the Bluth family
jokes.punchline: He uses a sports metaphor — 'put me in' — to argue for his place in Lucille's life, framing himself as a bench player who can contribute, which is both desperate and hilariously misplaced
jokes.explanation: A solid character moment — Tobias is so disconnected from normal social cues that he pitches himself like an athlete to his ex-mother-in-law. The sports framing is a good absurdist touch.

jokes.text: Oh, don't talk to me about walls. That's why we're in this mess. My husband buys land so we can get a contract to build a little something on the border. A wall.
jokes.setup: Tobias mentions 'walls' metaphorically (emotional walls Lucille builds)
jokes.punchline: Lucille takes it literally — she has a LITERAL wall problem because her husband secretly bought border land for a wall-building contract
jokes.explanation: The double meaning of 'walls' — psychological and physical — is well-executed. Lucille's pivot from therapy to real estate conspiracy is perfect for her character. The political resonance (border wall) adds a layer without being preachy.

jokes.text: I am gonna put this on my wall. [politician holding up a check while discussing the wall]
jokes.setup: The Bluths paid off a politician to push a wall-building agenda
jokes.punchline: The politician, shown in a flashback with a check, says 'I am gonna put this on my wall' — a pun on the wall/wall (border vs. hanging art) that the character delivers without irony
jokes.explanation: A clean double-meaning pun delivered in character. The politician's literal meaning (hanging the bribe check on his wall) alongside the wall-building context is well-constructed wordplay.

jokes.text: I bribed Love to get the wall, and I don't have the land to build it on!
jokes.setup: The scheme is being explained — George Sr. bribed a politician named 'Love' and bought land
jokes.punchline: He bribed someone named Love for something called a Wall, but bought the land on the wrong side of the border — a cascade of failures delivered as one compressed revelation
jokes.explanation: The absurdity of the scheme — bribed Love, got a Wall, on the wrong side — is compressed efficiently. The names (Love, Wall) feel like AD naming conventions. The structural failure is consistent with Bluth family competence.

jokes.text: It was just on the wrong side of the border. Which they might have known had George's brother Oscar not burned the signs. It's okay to burn these? Well, this is America. They don't want us to burn signs, they shouldn't write 'em in Spanish.
jokes.setup: The Bluths' land was on the Mexican side of the border; they didn't know because the signs were burned
jokes.punchline: Oscar burned them because they were in Spanish, applying American-pride logic to destroy property markers in Mexico
jokes.explanation: Oscar's xenophobic logic — burning Spanish signs in Spain/Mexico as a patriotic act — is a sharp satirical joke delivered with character specificity. 'This is America' applied to a Mexican location is absurdist irony. The economy of 'they shouldn't write 'em in Spanish' is excellent.

jokes.text: Who better than the Chinese to help us build a wall, right?
jokes.setup: The Bluths need financing for their border wall project
jokes.punchline: Lucille's 'solution' is to borrow from the Chinese — a reference to the Great Wall of China that also captures Lucille's casual racism and the Bluths' total lack of self-awareness
jokes.explanation: The Great Wall connection is the joke's foundation; Lucille's delivery as if this is obvious wisdom is the execution. Her character integration is high — this is exactly how she would think. Slightly broad but character-specific enough to work.

jokes.text: I can't be running a company that's building a wall I campaigned against!
jokes.setup: Lucille 2 discovers the Bluth Company is building the border wall
jokes.punchline: She campaigned against the wall — and now owns a majority stake in the company building it. The hypocrisy is the joke, but it's politically specific and character-driven
jokes.explanation: Political hypocrisy joke that works because it's character-specific (Lucille 2 as a politician) and plots-specific (her ownership stake). The irony is clean. Not exceptional but solid situational comedy.

jokes.text: hurt people... hurt people.
jokes.setup: Tobias delivers what he thinks is therapeutic wisdom
jokes.punchline: The phrase works two ways: 'hurt people [verb] people' (damaged people harm others) AND 'hurt [adjective] people' (people who are injured). Tobias delivers it as profound wisdom; the double meaning makes it either accidentally brilliant or accidentally stupid
jokes.explanation: A real therapeutic aphorism deployed by Tobias with appropriate gravitas. The double meaning — hurt people hurt people / hurt people (as in physically injured) — is a legitimate wordplay joke elevated by the character's earnestness. Economy is exceptional (three words). The callback later ('swimming!') elevates it further.

jokes.text: Oh, that's nice. I always say, 'Make people cry, make people cry.' But yours includes the people who don't want to give you the satisfaction.
jokes.setup: Tobias delivers 'hurt people hurt people' as wisdom
jokes.punchline: Lucille has her own competing aphorism — 'make people cry, make people cry' — which is just the same phrase twice with no double meaning, and she delivers it as equally wise
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional character comedy. 'Make people cry, make people cry' has no double meaning — it's just the same instruction twice — but Lucille presents it as a parallel aphorism. The addition 'but yours includes the people who don't want to give you the satisfaction' is a devastating character insight: Lucille sees making people cry as a goal with an acknowledged audience of reluctant participants. This is who Lucille Bluth is in two sentences. Character integration is nearly perfect. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Swimming! Hurt people, hurt people.
jokes.setup: The therapy session has just ended on the 'hurt people' aphorism
jokes.punchline: Tobias's non-sequitur 'Swimming!' — either a Freudian slip, a delayed free association, or a response to an emotional breakthrough — is followed by him repeating 'hurt people, hurt people,' suggesting the phrase has meant something to him personally
jokes.explanation: Tobias's random 'Swimming!' is a signature non-sequitur that reveals more than it explains. The callback to 'hurt people' suggests it resonated with him in a way that's both funny and slightly poignant — a small Tobias character moment.

jokes.text: Lindsay had also made it to Mexico, and was enjoying the fact that men were more respectful. Puta Night is Thursday. That's so nice. No, I'm not. That was based on her belief that 'puta' meant 'young.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay is enjoying what she thinks is respectful treatment from Mexican men
jokes.punchline: A bar sign reads 'Puta Night is Thursday'; Lindsay enthusiastically says 'that's so nice' and 'no, I'm not' — because she thinks 'puta' means 'young.' The narrator confirms her misconception.
jokes.explanation: The joke works on multiple levels: Lindsay's vanity, her ignorance, and her contradictory response ('that's so nice / no, I'm not'). The structure is elegant — the narrator's confirmation lands the punchline perfectly. Character integration is high; Lindsay's self-delusion is the engine.

jokes.text: No. Puta. I try to stay out of the sun.
jokes.setup: The bar worker tries to correct Lindsay about 'puta'
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's response — 'I try to stay out of the sun' — suggests she thinks they're discussing her skin tone/age, still not understanding what puta means
jokes.explanation: The escalation of Lindsay's misunderstanding — she now interprets the correction as being about sun damage aging her — is a perfect beat. Her vanity drives her further into the misunderstanding. Economy is excellent.

jokes.text: Oh, my God. Lindsay. What are you doing here? Guess I just had to get away. I was just ordering lunch. Can I buy you lunch? Oh, yeah. I'm so hungry, I could eat.
jokes.setup: Lindsay runs into what appears to be her father (George Sr.) — but it's actually Herbert Love, who resembles him
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's response to 'I'm so hungry' is 'I could eat' — a tautology that reads as Lindsay being so vapid she can't complete a sentence properly, or so unfazed she doesn't bother
jokes.explanation: The visual joke (Love resembling George Sr.) requires the visual to fully land; as dialogue it works moderately. 'I could eat' as a tautological completion is a mild joke. Rewatch bonus for the George Sr./Love resemblance subplot.

jokes.text: We are lucky. We have next to nothing. We are helping these people because nobody else will. They've been kicked out of the only country they've ever known because of the cruelty of your president... Barack Obama.
jokes.setup: A volunteer gives a passionate speech about displaced migrants, building to blame their president
jokes.punchline: The expected punchline in 2018-era context would be a Republican president; the volunteer says 'Barack Obama' — a reversal that works as both political observation and character joke
jokes.explanation: The setup leads the audience to expect a specific political punchline in 2018; the reversal to Obama is historically accurate (deportations during Obama era were high) and subversive. Good misdirection with real-world resonance.

jokes.text: So, you're trying to, like, teach them a lesson or...? We're volunteers. Who did you think the water was for? Oh. I thought you were just trying to get me to give you a higher Uber rating.
jokes.setup: Volunteers are giving water to migrants; Maeby misreads the situation
jokes.punchline: Maeby thought they were doing a service that required a good Uber rating — reducing humanitarian aid to a gig economy interaction
jokes.explanation: Sharp observational comedy about millennial/Gen-Z social reality — Maeby's frame of reference is gig economy service transactions, so humanitarian volunteers read to her as Uber drivers optimizing for ratings. The joke captures something true about social atomization. Character integration is solid for Maeby's self-centered worldview. Justifies 8+ for originality and cultural sharpness.

jokes.text: Mexico. It's like being in the Spanish dorms of the UCI sister campus in Spain all over again.
jokes.setup: George Michael reflects on his Mexico experience
jokes.punchline: He compares Mexico to 'the Spanish dorms of the UCI sister campus in Spain' — a statement that reveals he has no concept of authentic cultural experience; his frame of reference is a California university's foreign dormitories
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'UCI sister campus in Spain' as his reference point is a sharp character joke — George Michael's limited worldview means his most exotic comparison is still a domestic university's foreign program. Captures his sheltered Orange County upbringing.

jokes.text: God, the salt is different here. It's fatter or something, right? We're from Orange County.
jokes.setup: George Michael observes the cuisine in Mexico
jokes.punchline: 'The salt is fatter' is a wonderfully specific non-observation; the immediate 'we're from Orange County' from another American explains the cultural myopia without needing elaboration
jokes.explanation: 'Fatter salt' is a perfect absurdist observation — specific enough to be a real thing a sheltered person might say. 'We're from Orange County' does all the work of explanation in four words. Good economy.

jokes.text: They saw foreign films. I'm glad someone had the guts to give the Minions their own movie. It's great because it's subtitled in Spanish, but we don't have to read. It's in English. If you don't want to see 'em, you can block 'em.
jokes.setup: The Americans are watching 'foreign films' in Mexico
jokes.punchline: Their 'foreign film' is Minions — an American film they're watching because it's subtitled in Spanish (so technically foreign-language for the subtitles) but English audio. Then a Fakeblock callback: 'If you don't want to see 'em, you can block 'em.'
jokes.explanation: The 'foreign film' twist (Minions) is good; the Fakeblock callback is a clean, understated connection. The irony of calling Minions a 'foreign film' because of Spanish subtitles is sharp cultural satire.

jokes.text: They discovered charming native communities where local artisans peddled their wares. [cut to: Señor Frog's or equivalent American-style tourist bar/shop in Mexico]
jokes.setup: The narrator describes the Americans discovering authentic local culture
jokes.punchline: The visual cut reveals the 'charming native community' is a tourist-trap American-style establishment, undercutting the narrator's romantic description
jokes.explanation: A visual gag inferred from the narrator's pompous setup. The juxtaposition of elevated 'local artisans' language with an obvious tourist trap is a reliable irony structure. Works well as a quick cut.

jokes.text: Adopted customs like the midday break, or siesta. Yes! Who left the air conditioning on? Oh, me. I never left.
jokes.setup: The narrator says the Americans 'adopted' the siesta custom
jokes.punchline: Their 'siesta' involves turning off the AC and complaining about it; one of them never left their air-conditioned room in the first place — the opposite of cultural immersion
jokes.explanation: The anti-immersion joke (never leaving the air conditioning) is a consistent beat in the Americans-in-Mexico sequence. Not exceptional but steady.

jokes.text: Candidate descended the escalator into the lobby. I will build a great, great wall on our southern border. Which was my idea! And I will have Mexico pay for that wall. Okay. That is a clever twist.
jokes.setup: Lucille is watching TV; a political candidate makes a speech about building a wall
jokes.punchline: Lucille's only objection is that it's HER idea being stolen; her concession that 'having Mexico pay for it' is 'a clever twist' treats massive political demagogy as a business negotiation detail she's impressed by
jokes.explanation: Lucille's response to a Trump-era speech is perfectly in character: she's not appalled by the politics, she's irritated about intellectual property theft of her corrupt scheme. 'That is a clever twist' applied to 'Mexico pays for it' is devastating character comedy — she's evaluating the speech as a business pitch, not political rhetoric. Character integration is near-perfect. Justifies 8+ score.

jokes.text: Michael had been at Search for a month. In that time, he'd developed himself physically... And then lunge. Duck, sweep the leg, lunge.
jokes.setup: The narrator says Michael has 'developed himself physically' during his month at the tech company
jokes.punchline: The physical development is a self-defense class where Michael teaches 'duck, sweep the leg, lunge' — Karate Kid-level moves in a corporate wellness context
jokes.explanation: A pleasant absurdist detail — Michael's version of 'developing himself physically' is teaching fight choreography at a tech campus. Works as character comedy; not exceptional.

jokes.text: Ted, I think you see your daughter coming. You're not expecting this, but she's coming in hot with a right... He'd gotten to teach a class he'd successfully persuaded management to try out. Mona, I know your husband's down, he probably needs medical attention, but you got another son on your flank.
jokes.setup: Michael is teaching at the tech company
jokes.punchline: Michael's class turns out to be simulated family attack scenarios ('Mom Attacks Son,' 'Dad Attacks Daughter') — he's teaching corporate employees to defend against their own families, based entirely on his personal life
jokes.explanation: Michael projecting his family trauma into a corporate defense curriculum is a sharp character joke. The specificity of the scenarios (Mom Attacks Son, Dad Attacks Daughter, Dad Attacks Mom) reveals his personal obsessions. Naming real class participants ('Ted,' 'Mona') is a good realism detail. Justifies 8 with character integration.

jokes.text: And everybody, we're going to duck, sweep the leg, and lunge. And he comes back again. And we sweep the leg and lunge. That's great. Everybody put down your cactus. Let's all huddle up.
jokes.setup: Michael is running the family defense class
jokes.punchline: 'Everybody put down your cactus' — delivered casually as if having a cactus in a corporate defense class is standard — is the absurdist detail that elevates the sequence
jokes.explanation: 'Put down your cactus' is a perfect AD non-sequitur: unexplained, casual, and utterly specific. The economy is excellent — four words that imply an entire world of corporate defense training absurdity.

jokes.text: Wow, satisfy an old dream. Boy, do you really have to spend 1,000 bucks on the gear, though? Not if you have your own. Mm. Yeah. Well, not here, but I could drive home and get it. Course, I could see my family.
jokes.setup: Michael has found 'a way to satisfy an old dream' at the tech company
jokes.punchline: The 'old dream' — revealed to require $1,000 in gear — turns out to be something he already owns equipment for at home. His realization that going home means seeing his family is a delayed comic beat on his avoidance
jokes.explanation: A functional character joke about Michael's avoidance. The realization that seeing his gear means seeing his family is a good beat but execution is slightly meandering. The 'old dream' mystery is never quite delivered as a proper punchline.

jokes.text: Wow, satisfy an old dream. Boy, do you really have to spend 1,000 bucks on the gear, though? / Not if you have your own.
jokes.setup: Michael discovers a way to satisfy a dream at the tech campus
jokes.punchline: The 'old dream' requiring expensive gear (presumably something athletic or recreational) is met with 'not if you have your own' — implying Michael could have had this all along and wasted the opportunity
jokes.explanation: The joke is underdeveloped because the 'dream' is never specified clearly. The structure relies on context the transcript doesn't fully provide. Weaker moment.

jokes.text: Hola. Window cleaner!
jokes.setup: Michael sneaks into the model home disguised to avoid being recognized
jokes.punchline: His disguise is calling himself a 'window cleaner' in a fake Spanish accent — an absurdly transparent disguise for a man whose family lives there
jokes.explanation: Classic bad-disguise comedy. Works because Michael is terrible at deception and yet tries anyway. The 'Hola' Spanish accent opener is a good character detail — Michael code-switching badly.

jokes.text: I shall be neither seen nor heard. I blue myself. It's vodka. It goes bad once it's opened. I heard the jury's still out on science.
jokes.setup: Michael arrives at the model home; Buster and Tobias are present
jokes.punchline: Multiple jokes compressed: Buster invokes the Milford motto ('neither seen nor heard'), Tobias has apparently painted himself blue again ('I blue myself'), and explains it with 'I heard the jury's still out on science' — a callback to the original 'never nude' and blue-painting gags
jokes.explanation: A dense callback cluster. 'I blue myself' is one of the show's most beloved lines; its reuse here earns high marks from fans. 'I heard the jury's still out on science' is a callback to George Sr.'s line from Season 1. The compression of multiple beloved callbacks into one scene is quintessential AD.

jokes.text: Until right at the end there. [Tobias's blue paint seen by the family — his disguise as window cleaner fails at the crucial moment]
jokes.setup: Tobias has been pretending to be a window cleaner alongside Michael
jokes.punchline: His disguise works until the very end — where the blue paint apparently gives him away. Michael's 'until right at the end there' is a resigned acknowledgment of Tobias's characteristic failure
jokes.explanation: A good button on the blue Tobias sequence. Michael's deadpan 'until right at the end there' is a classic Michael understatement. The visual of blue Tobias failing at the crucial moment is inferred.

jokes.text: She forgave the debt. And feeling bad that he'd been forgiven by someone he wanted to forget, he decided to thank her in person.
jokes.setup: Michael finds a letter from Lucille 2
jokes.punchline: He 'feels bad' about being forgiven — not because he's grateful, but because forgiveness from someone he's been avoiding creates a social obligation he can't escape. The narrator's framing reveals Michael's selfish emotional logic.
jokes.explanation: The irony of feeling bad about being forgiven is a sharp character insight — Michael's self-centeredness frames even grace as an imposition. The narrator's dry framing ('someone he wanted to forget') is efficient and cutting.

jokes.text: Speech! Speech! You're my third least favorite child.
jokes.setup: Michael arrives at what appears to be a family gathering
jokes.punchline: Lucille's greeting after not seeing her son for months is 'You're my third least favorite child' — not third favorite, but third LEAST favorite, suggesting a precise internal ranking of which children she dislikes least
jokes.explanation: A standout joke. The construction is precise: she says 'third least favorite' not 'third favorite' — the negative framing means she's in the bottom tier of disfavor, which is as close to approval as Lucille gets. It also implies she's done the math on all her children's rankings. Character integration is as high as it gets — this is pure Lucille. Economy is perfect. Quotability is among the highest in the episode. Justifies 8+ score.

jokes.text: Like a Motherboy Ball.
jokes.setup: The family has gathered; the narrator describes the scene
jokes.punchline: 'Like a Motherboy Ball' — referencing the show's recurring Motherboy pageant, which is itself a joke about Buster and Lucille's inappropriate co-dependence
jokes.explanation: A quick, clean callback that rewards longtime viewers. The comparison to Motherboy contextualizes the family reunion as another version of the Bluth's twisted dynamic. Compact and effective.

jokes.text: I should ask if she wants to get pre-pre-engaged.
jokes.setup: Michael has just returned; in the model home, he's reconnecting with family
jokes.punchline: 'Pre-pre-engaged' is two stages before engaged — Michael's romantic timidity expressed through bureaucratic commitment escalation
jokes.explanation: The escalation of commitment stages (pre-pre-engaged) is a good Michael joke — his inability to commit expressed through adding pre-commitment stages. Solid character comedy.

jokes.text: I want that pie covered. And not with your face.
jokes.setup: The family is together; Lucille is in domestic mode
jokes.punchline: Lucille's pie instruction doubles as an insult — 'not with your face' implies someone's face is comparable to (or worse than) a pie cover, delivered as practical instruction
jokes.explanation: Classic Lucille insult: practical framing that delivers a devastating personal attack. 'Not with your face' is a perfectly economical Lucille line — cutting and specific. Character integration is high.

jokes.text: G.O.B.! I haven't seen you in a year! It's been two months. Get your hands off. Oh, yeah? What's the last thing you remember us doing? What do you remember? What? No. Enough!
jokes.setup: Michael reunites with G.O.B.; both have lost track of their shared timeline
jokes.punchline: G.O.B.'s defensive 'what do you remember?' implies something happened between them that he's hoping Michael doesn't remember — a classic G.O.B. guilty deflection
jokes.explanation: G.O.B.'s guilty deflection works as character comedy — his 'what do you remember?' implies something embarrassing happened (possibly related to the forget-me-now drug). Rewatch bonus for the season arc context.

jokes.text: Harmless bathhousing amongst brothers, yes?
jokes.setup: The brothers are sorting out what happened between them
jokes.punchline: 'Harmless bathhousing amongst brothers' — G.O.B. frames whatever happened in the most innocent-sounding terms that are simultaneously the most suspicious possible. 'Bathhousing' implies a bathhouse; 'amongst brothers' is doing enormous work
jokes.explanation: One of the strongest jokes in the episode. 'Harmless bathhousing amongst brothers' is a phrase that tries to normalize something clearly not normal. The word 'bathhousing' is doing extraordinary work — it's simultaneously innocent (bathing) and loaded (bathhouse culture). G.O.B.'s delivery of it as a casual reassurance is pitch-perfect character comedy. Economy is exceptional. Quotability is among the highest in the episode. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: And George Michael dips his toe into the Mexican nightlife. Guys, it's 9:00. Gives us 30 minutes till they shut down that last machine at Pinkberry.
jokes.setup: The narrator promises Mexican nightlife for George Michael
jokes.punchline: George Michael's 'nightlife' is making it to the Pinkberry (a frozen yogurt chain) before the last machine shuts down at 9:30 PM — a crushing deflation of the 'nightlife' promise
jokes.explanation: The episode closes on a perfect character joke: George Michael's 'Mexican nightlife' is a frozen yogurt run before 9:30. The gap between the narrator's promise and the reality is maximally wide. Pinkberry as a specific brand makes it sharper. The time-sensitive urgency ('30 minutes') makes it funnier. A strong closing joke that encapsulates George Michael's character. Justifies 8+ for structure and character integration.

tmdb_overview: For varying reasons, most of the Bluths (even Steve Holt) feel compelled to run off to Mexico. Michael tries to convince Buster to go to the police.
insight: Absurdist plotting carries a mid-tier episode through 59 jokes in steady, character-driven rhythms.
